The witnesses note if ye continue in the faith grounded and settled. The witnesses note be not moved away from the hope of the gospel.

The lesson the expositors draw is that continuing in the faith, grounded and unmoved, is the evidence and path of those truly reconciled. 24:
"Who now rejoice in my sufferings for you, and fill up that which is behind of the afflictions of Christ in my flesh for his body's sake, which is the church:" The witnesses note Paul's joy in his sufferings and the filling up of Christ's afflictions. Gill notes Paul, as soon as he mentions his being a minister, speaks of his sufferings, which always attend such an office — appointed by God, foretold by Christ, necessary, and to be patiently endured. The witnesses note Paul declaring that he now rejoices in his sufferings for them, filling up what is behind of the afflictions of Christ in his flesh for the church's sake; the joyful sharing in Christ's afflictions for the church — that Paul, far from grieving over his imprisonment and trials, rejoices in them as endured "for" the Colossians, and speaks of "filling up that which is behind of the afflictions of Christ" — not the atoning sufferings of Christ (which are complete and need no supplement), but the sufferings that Christ's members endure for his sake, which Christ counts as his own (since the head suffers in the body), the appointed measure of which the apostle bears his share in his own flesh for the good of the church, Christ's body; fill up that which is behind of the afflictions of Christ. The witnesses note who now rejoice in my sufferings for you. The witnesses note fill up that which is behind of the afflictions of Christ in my flesh for his body's sake.

25:
"Whereof I am made a minister, according to the dispensation of God which is given to me for you, to fulfil the word of God;" The witnesses note Paul's stewardship of the gospel for the church. Gill notes Paul was made a minister of the church (for whose sake he endured afflictions), not its saviour or lord, but its servant, who ministered to it in holy things, in the word and ordinances. The witnesses note Paul declaring that he was made a minister of the church according to the dispensation of God given him for them, to fulfil the word of God; the divine stewardship to make the word fully known — that Paul describes his ministry as a "dispensation" (a stewardship, an office of trust) given him by God for the church's sake, the charge being "to fulfil the word of God" — that is, fully to preach and make known the whole counsel of God, completing and carrying out to the full the message entrusted to him, so that his apostolic office was a sacred trust for the benefit of the church, discharged by the full proclamation of God's truth; the dispensation of God which is given to me for you, to fulfil the word of God. The witnesses note whereof I am made a minister, according to the dispensation of God. The witnesses note to fulfil the word of God.

26:
"Even the mystery which hath been hid from ages and from generations, but now is made manifest to his saints:" The witnesses note the mystery once hidden, now revealed to the saints. Chrysostom notes Paul, having shown the lovingkindness of God by the greatness of the gift, heightens it further: that none before knew this — not angels, nor principalities, nor any created power, but only the Son of God; and he says not simply "hid" but "quite hid." The witnesses note Paul speaking of the mystery hidden from ages and generations, but now made manifest to his saints; the long-concealed mystery now unveiled to God's people — that Paul magnifies the word he was charged to fulfil as a "mystery": a truth (the gospel, and chiefly the Gentiles' full inclusion in Christ) which had been "hid from ages and from generations" — kept secret through all the long course of time past, unknown even to the angelic world — but which is "now made manifest to his saints," openly unveiled in the gospel age to the people of God, so that what was once a deep secret is now the open possession of believers; hid from ages and generations, but now is made manifest. The witnesses note the mystery which hath been hid from ages and from generations. The witnesses note but now is made manifest to his saints.

The witnesses note the mystery hidden from ages, now made manifest to the saints — a truth long kept secret through all time past, now openly unveiled to God's people.

So the witnesses make the verse the once-hidden mystery now revealed. The lesson the expositors draw is that the gospel-mystery, kept secret through all past ages, is now openly unveiled to the saints. 27:
"To whom God would make known what is the riches of the glory of this mystery among the Gentiles; which is Christ in you, the hope of glory:" The witnesses note the glorious mystery — Christ in the believer, the hope of glory. Gill notes the spring and cause of the manifestation of the gospel to the saints is not their works, nor any preparations in them, nor a foresight of their improvement, but God's own sovereign grace and will. The witnesses note Paul declaring that God would make known the riches of the glory of this mystery among the Gentiles, which is Christ in them, the hope of glory; the indwelling Christ as the hope of glory — that Paul names the very heart and treasure of the mystery: the "riches of the glory" of it, displayed especially "among the Gentiles" (that they too should share in Christ), and sums it up in a phrase of unmatched preciousness — "Christ in you, the hope of glory" — that is, Christ himself, dwelling in the believer's heart by his Spirit and faith, who is the ground, pledge, and sure hope of the coming glory, so that the indwelling Christ is at once the believer's present treasure and his guarantee of heaven; Christ in you, the hope of glory. The witnesses note the riches of the glory of this mystery among the Gentiles. The witnesses note which is Christ in you, the hope of glory.

The witnesses note the heart of the mystery — "Christ in you, the hope of glory" — the indwelling Christ the believer's present treasure and his sure pledge of coming glory.

The verse thus records the indwelling Christ as the hope of glory — teaching that the treasure of the mystery is Christ dwelling in the believer, the ground and pledge of coming glory.
28:
"Whom we preach, warning every man, and teaching every man in all wisdom; that we may present every man perfect in Christ Jesus:" The witnesses note the preaching of Christ to bring every man to perfection in him. Gill notes Christ — the riches, glory, and mystery of the gospel, the hope set before lost sinners, the only Saviour, by whose righteousness believers are justified and through whose blood their sins are pardoned — was the subject Paul preached. The witnesses note Paul declaring that they preach Christ, warning and teaching every man in all wisdom, that they may present every man perfect in Christ Jesus; the universal aim of the ministry to perfect every man in Christ — that Paul sums up his ministry: its one subject is Christ ("whom we preach"), its method is faithful "warning" (admonishing of sin and danger) and "teaching" (instructing in the truth) and that "in all wisdom" (with all prudence and the whole counsel of God), and its goal is universal and complete — "to present every man perfect in Christ Jesus," that every soul under his care might be brought to maturity and completeness in Christ, lacking nothing; that we may present every man perfect in Christ Jesus. The witnesses note whom we preach, warning every man, and teaching every man. The witnesses note that we may present every man perfect in Christ Jesus.

The witnesses note the preaching of Christ — warning and teaching every man — to present every man perfect in Christ Jesus; the universal aim to bring all to completeness in him.

So the witnesses make the verse the aim to perfect every man in Christ. The lesson the expositors draw is that the ministry preaches Christ, warning and teaching, to bring every soul to completeness in him. The verse thus records the aim to perfect every man in Christ — teaching that the ministry preaches Christ, warning and teaching, to bring every soul to completeness in him.
29:
"Whereunto I also labour, striving according to his working, which worketh in me mightily." The witnesses note Paul's strenuous labour by the power of Christ working in him. Gill notes Paul laboured in the word and doctrine, preaching Christ, warning sinners, teaching the way of salvation, with the view that in the great day he might bring a large number and set them before Christ as the seals of his ministry. Barnes notes "striving" is literally "agonizing." The witnesses note Paul declaring that for this end he labours, striving according to the working of Christ which works mightily in him; the toil of the apostle empowered by Christ's might — that Paul, having named the goal of presenting every man perfect, owns the cost: he "labours" (toils to weariness) and "strives" (agonizes, as in a contest, with intense effort), and yet not in his own strength, but "according to his working, which worketh in me mightily" — that is, by the mighty energy of Christ working powerfully within him, so that all his strenuous effort is itself the fruit and operation of the indwelling power of Christ; striving according to his working, which worketh in me mightily. The witnesses note whereunto I also labour, striving. The witnesses note according to his working, which worketh in me mightily.

The witnesses note Paul's strenuous labour — toiling and agonizing, yet not in his own strength but by the mighty working of Christ within him.

So the witnesses make the verse Paul's labour empowered by Christ's might. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the whereunto I also labour striving, the according to his working which worketh in me mightily, and the labour empowered by Christ. The verse thus records Paul's labour empowered by Christ's might — teaching that Paul's strenuous toil is itself the fruit of Christ's mighty power working within him.
